post: "No taxation without representation" is famous. But representation was being denied long before the tax debates. Grievance 3 of the Declaration of Independence targets a quieter form of disenfranchisement: colonial districts created but denied any voice in their own legislatures. The policy, engineered by King George III and Lord Hillsborough, was designed to limit colonial expansion, and, with it, colonial political power. Jefferson had seen it clearly by 1774. Without representation, he wrote, Americans would be subject to "the absolute slaves of the king's sovereign will." By 1776, "Farmer George" had a new name. Zachary Deibel, assistant professor of history at VMI, unpacks Grievance 3 in Episode 3 of our 27-part series. The four-year college degree has long been treated as the default pathway to professional success. And for many students, it still is. Degree holders earn higher average lifetime incomes and face lower unemployment rates than those without a bachelor's degree. But tuition has risen sharply. Student loan debt has become a defining financial reality for millions of graduates. And the economy has opened meaningful alternative pathways  trade schools, apprenticeships, certifications, and direct workforce entry  that didn't exist at the same scale a generation ago. Think the Vote's final debate of the school year: Is a four-year college degree worth it today? Submissions close May 21. End strong. 👉  https://bit.ly/4tlNCv4  #CivicsEducation  #ThinktheVote  #CollegeDebt  #SocialStudies  #BRI  #CareerReadiness

The future of civic education gets reimagined in rooms like this one. Last week, the Civics Innovation Lab gathered educators, community leaders, and technology partners at the Museum of History and Industry in Seattle for an evening that was equal parts inspiration and action. The panelists and dinner conversations were insightful, and the Cocktail Innovation Lab didn't hurt either. What we heard from attendees confirmed what we've hearing from similar events around the country: the future of civics will be co-created together, through community engagement and local partners. Every community faces unique challenges, and so every community needs unique solutions. Language barriers, digital divides, and rural isolation look different both within Washington state and across the country. A one-size-fits-all approach won't solve inequitable access or civic disengagement. That's why the Civics Innovation Lab is working with local partners like we met in Seattle: actively listening to what diverse populations need, collaborating on community-first solutions, and helping young people break old paradigms that limit civic participation. If you believe the next generation deserves better civic education, and the tools to match, we'd love to have you in the conversation. 🔗  https://bit.ly/48ErdS4  #CivicEducation  #Civics  #EdTech  #CivicsInnovation  #Seattle  #BRI

What happens when a government stops doing its job? For the colonists of 1776, that wasn't a hypothetical. King George III was blocking colonial laws from taking effect, and then failing to approve or reject them at all. The result: colonial governance ground to a halt. Enlightenment thinkers had an answer for this moment. Dr. Susan Brynne Long explains why, in Grievance 2 of the Declaration, the colonists weren't just frustrated. They believed they were obligated to respond. Episode 2 is live. Follow along for every grievance:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #USHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #AmericanRevolution

Why has civic education taught students to look to Washington, when citizenship starts in their own neighborhood? In Season 3, Episode 19 of 𝙏𝙝𝙞𝙨 𝘾𝙤𝙣𝙨𝙩𝙞𝙩𝙪𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣, host Matthew Brogdon sits down with David Bobb, president of the Bill of Rights Institute, to explore the state of civic education in America as the country approaches its 250th birthday. 27 grievances. 27 videos. 1 revolution. The Declaration of Independence is 800+ years of political philosophy compressed into a single document. From the Magna Carta (1215) to John Locke to Jefferson's committee of five, the Founders were building on centuries of ideas about rights, law, and what governments owe the people they govern. Dr. C. C. Borzilleri kicks off a new 27-part series by unpacking the question at the heart of the Declaration: what happens when a government stops doing the very thing it exists to do? Episode 1 is live. New videos drop every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #USHistory  #HistoryTeachers  #A250
